title: SSRF vulnerability in gitlab.com webhook
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
expected_evidence: application stores attacker-controlled webhook URL and later performs a server-side request when the event fires
description: Public report summary indicates GitLab project webhook configuration accepted a URL that later caused SSRF when integration events were delivered.
hints: configure target URL first
trigger an outbound event later
extraction_notes: endpoint and parameters inferred from public report title/summary

title: SSRF vulnerability in gitlab.com via project import
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
expected_evidence: application stores a remote project import source and later fetches it server-side, allowing internal addresses through validation bypass
description: Public report summary describes a bypass in project import URL validation that enabled SSRF during import execution.
hints: look for import source registration then execution
test decimal/octal/alternate loopback forms

title: Blind SSRF in FogBugz project import
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
expected_evidence: configured FogBugz import later causes a blind server-side request to attacker-controlled or internal destinations
description: Public report title explicitly describes blind SSRF during a FogBugz project import flow.
hints: import config is first phase
OAST or internal-host side effects appear later
extraction_notes: details inferred from title and common FogBugz import shape

title: FogBugz import attachment full SSRF requiring vulnerability in *.fogbugz.com
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
expected_evidence: attachment import workflow later dereferences a stored external URL or host and can reach internal targets
description: Public report summary indicates FogBugz attachment import caused full SSRF against remote hosts.
hints: file/attachment import sources often fetch later during processing
extraction_notes: flow and params inferred from title

title: Arbitrary file read during project import
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
expected_evidence: project import configuration causes the backend to read arbitrary local or packaged files during later processing
description: Public report title explicitly indicates arbitrary file read during project import processing.
hints: configuration and execution are separate phases
look for later preview or import result containing file contents
extraction_notes: exact read primitive not public in snippet

title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via Analytics Reports
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
expected_evidence: report generation or analytics processing later performs a server-side request to attacker-controlled URLs
description: Public report summary says analytics reports could be abused for SSRF, implying a delayed processing sink.
hints: report rendering often happens asynchronously
look for URL-backed assets or templates
extraction_notes: path and parameters inferred from title

title: SSRF in Next.js Image component
expected_evidence: server-side image optimization endpoint fetches attacker-controlled URLs supplied through the url parameter
description: Intigriti documents that Next.js image optimization exposes /_next/image and the url parameter is a strong SSRF testing point.
hints: test url= with internal metadata targets
extraction_notes: endpoint and parameter explicitly named in article

title: SSRF in Next.js Middleware (CVE-2025-57822)
vuln_class_raw: Middleware SSRF via request header processing
expected_evidence: middleware evaluates attacker-controlled header data and causes a backend fetch to the supplied location
description: Intigriti shows a vulnerable Next.js middleware pattern where an arbitrary Location header can make the framework load attacker-chosen resources.
hints: header-driven SSRF may need a second request/redirect chain
extraction_notes: article names the Location header pattern

title: SSRF via Next.js Server Actions (CVE-2024-34351)
expected_evidence: server action performs a HEAD then GET flow influenced by attacker-controlled host/redirect handling, enabling SSRF to internal targets
description: Intigriti documents a multi-step SSRF in server actions involving a HEAD request, content-type validation, and redirect chaining.
hints: requires two-step interaction and redirect handling
watch for host header influence

title: Dynamic Client Registration: SSRF by design
expected_evidence: dynamic client registration stores attacker-controlled metadata that is later dereferenced by the authorization service, causing SSRF
description: PortSwigger research introduced dynamic client registration as an OAuth/OIDC SSRF vector by design.
hints: client metadata such as logo_uri or jwks_uri may be dereferenced later
extraction_notes: research article provides generic vector rather than single target

title: redirect_uri Session Poisoning
expected_evidence: authorization flow persists an attacker-influenced redirect_uri or session value that is later used to send the victim or code to an unintended location
description: PortSwigger research identifies redirect_uri session poisoning as a second-order OAuth/OIDC issue.
hints: look for stored redirect_uri values reused in later authorization steps
extraction_notes: generic research pattern normalized to auth flow

title: OAuth redirect_uri bypass using IDN homograph attack
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
expected_evidence: authorization service accepts a maliciously encoded redirect_uri and later sends the user or authorization code to an attacker-controlled callback
description: Public report summary explicitly says redirect_uri validation was bypassed using an IDN homograph attack.
hints: test exact-match edge cases and encoded look-alike hosts
extraction_notes: validation bypass type explicit in summary

title: Oauth flow on the comments widget leaks authorization code via redirect_uri
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
expected_evidence: valid OAuth authorization code is delivered to external hosts due to insufficient redirect_uri validation
description: Public report summary explicitly notes authorization code leakage to external hosts through insufficient redirect_uri validation.
hints: look for widget/embed OAuth flows with relaxed callback validation

title: [Critical] - Steal OAuth Tokens
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
vuln_class_raw: OAuth token theft due to misconfiguration
expected_evidence: OAuth tokens or codes can be redirected to attacker-controlled destinations due to provider/client misconfiguration
description: Public report summary states OAuth tokens could be stolen because of a misconfiguration in the auth flow.
hints: compare provider-specific callback allowlists and error paths
extraction_notes: summary is high-level; exact primitive not fully public

title: Login email verification bypass via /oauth/token
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
vuln_class_raw: Email verification bypass in OAuth token flow
expected_evidence: token issuance flow bypasses email verification or related login checks when using a supported grant type
description: Public report title explicitly points to /oauth/token as the path used to bypass email verification during login.
hints: resource-owner password or alternate grant flows may skip higher-level checks

title: Account takeover due to previously used Bitbucket login/import linkage
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
vuln_class_raw: Account takeover through imported/linked identity reuse
expected_evidence: previously linked import or login identity is reused in a later authorization step, allowing takeover or cross-account binding
description: Public report summary describes account takeover when a user had previously used Bitbucket login or import with GitLab.
hints: linked provider state may persist across flows
import and login may share identity binding logic

title: Stealing Users OAuth authorization code via redirect_uri
license_or_reuse_note: public disclosed report metadata and summary
vuln_class_raw: Authorization code theft via redirect_uri path traversal
expected_evidence: a crafted redirect_uri path or traversal trick causes authorization codes to be sent to attacker-controlled content or destinations
description: Public report summary says a path traversal issue in redirect_uri allowed users’ OAuth authorization codes to be stolen.
hints: normalize callback paths before validation
treat path confusion as callback validation issue
extraction_notes: summary explicit about redirect_uri path traversal
